title: [Feature request] Intermediate values in bbob-mixint
author: TGlas
body:
**Describe the feature**
In mixed-integer linear programming it is possible to evaluate the objective function at non-integer values, even if the final solution shall be constrained to integers. That is actually even a requirement for branch-and-bound solvers to be applicable. Of course, I can evaluate at non-integers, but then I get the function value of the truncated point, which is not useful for branch-and-bound.

**Rationale.** While surely there exist problems for which the objective is undefined for non-integer values, there are likely many others, for which the situation is more like in MILP. Therefore, I would find it useful to equip BBOB-mixint with the ability to evaluate at non-integer coordinates.

**Approach.** Such a change would likely require an ask-tell-propose interface, where only the propose part is restricted to integer solutions.

title: Using transformations from COCO
author: kvdblom
body:
We (@CIGbalance and I) plan to use performance trajectories in our work, and would like to normalise them for generalisation purposes. To this end, we would like to test our normalisation strategy against common transformations, and thought we might be able to do that with code from COCO.

What we want to do:
- Run on a base function, get a trajectory of x,y values out
- Apply transformations as done in COCO, get a second trajectory of transformed x,y values
- Use some normalisation strategy on both trajectories
- If it is a good normalisation strategy, both trajectories should be the same after normalisation

What we are unsure about is the x values. We think they are also transformed appropriately, i.e. with x-shifts of the optima. In this case, (we think) this approach makes sense. If they aren't, we don't expect this to work.

This leads to some questions:
1. Do the transformations (particularly on x values) work as we hope?
2. Are there other issues with this approach that we might be overlooking?
3. How would we practically go about getting those two trajectories out of COCO? (e.g., which functions would we need to call to apply the transformations?)

title: postprocessing plots explanation
author: Luisenden
body:
I have a question regarding the 'plus' marker in some of the graphs-- what does it represent? E.g. here https://coco-platform.org/getting-started/images/CMA-E_NELDE_SLSQP_pprldmany_f006_20D.svg

While the 'cross' is very well explained as the median of maximum run lengths aggregated in these [docs](https://numbbo.github.io/coco-doc/perf-assessment/), I could not find the 'plus' sign explained anywhere (searched the paper and [coco API](https://numbbo.github.io/coco-doc/apidocs/cocoex/index.html)).

title: Request optimal point
author: troyrock
body: I have my optimization code running on the bbob suite, however I don't know how well I'm doing unless I know the function value of the optimal point.  Is there a way to request the optimum function value so that I can see if my method was successful?  Thanks.

Entering `python do.py run-c` results in: can't open file '<directory>/coco/do.py': [Errno 2] No such file or directory

title: [Bug report] cocoprep module not usable anymore

body:
**Describe the bug**
We did not take care of moving the `cocoprep` module to the new installation procedures like we did for `cocopp` and `cocoex`. Without the now-outdated `do.py`, it is not possible to install the module anymore (or at least I don't know how to do).

title: [Bug report] compatibility of cocoex with numpy 2.0
author: nikohansen
body:
**Describe the bug**
`cocoex` bailes with `numpy` 2.0, due to [this code](https://github.com/numbbo/coco/commit/12c2e374cbf317f55eb67c477c7881fbec798701) which is now fixed. I didn't check yet whether it works now.

We should have a test checking that the package is compatible with `numpy` 2.0?

title: [Feature request] Runtime distribution aggregated on a chosen set of functions
author: nikohansen
body:
We should have a recipe for how to create/show runtime distributions for a user-defined subset of the functions of a testbed. For example, all graphs and html pages could be created from an initially reduced/filtered data set list.

Usecases:
- we want to see aggregated performance over all unimodal or all nonseparable unimodal functions (the current function groups make an additional distinction by condition number).
- we want to see aggregated performance over all functions except for `f5`, see https://github.com/numbbo/coco/issues/2304#issuecomment-2226854715 

This could be implemented as another filter (like the current `Testbed.filter` method) which reads a global option defining the subset, for example a `Testbed.user_filter` method which reads a `cocopp.genericsettings.function_ids_filter` variable where negative values remove the respective function.

title: [DATA SUBMISSION] DE-NO-r1-B, DE-j-r2-B, and DE-CoBi-r1-L on the bbob-mixint suite
author: ryojitanabe
body:
Dear Organizers of COCO,

I would like to submit the benchmarking results of three optimizers (DE-NO-r1-B, DE-j-r2-B, and DE-CoBi-r1-L) on the bbob-mixint suite. Could you please upload them to the COCO data archive?

Best regards,
Ryoji

#### Reference
Ryoji Tanabe: Benchmarking Parameter Control Methods in Differential Evolution for Mixed-Integer Black-Box Optimization, Proc. ACM Genetic and Evolutionary Computation Conference (GECCO2024), [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.03303)

#### Description of the Algorithm(s)

The above paper benchmarked parameter control methods for scale factor and crossover rate in differential evolution (DE) on the bbob-mixint suite. The results show that using a suitable parameter control method significantly improves the performance of DE on the bbob-mixint suite.
I benchmarked 160 DE configurations, where I used 10 parameter control methods, 8 mutation strategies, and 2 repair methods. However, uploading the benchmarking data of the 160 optimizers to the COCO data archive is not possible. For this reason, I would like submit only the benchmarking results of the following three optimizers. Here, in the notation "DE-X-Y-Z", X represents the type of parameter control method, Y represents the type of mutation strategy, and Z represents the type of repair method.

- **DE-NO-r1-B**: DE with no parameter control method, the rand/1 strategy, and Baldwinian repair method. This is the most classical DE algorithm.
- **DE-j-r2-B**: DE with the parameter control method in [jDE](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/4016057), the rand/2 strategy, and Baldwinian repair method. This DE performs the best out of the 160 DE configurations for 160 dimensions.
- **DE-CoBi-r1-L**: DE with the parameter control method in [CoBiDE](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1568494614000581), the rand/1 strategy, and Lamarckian repair method. This DE performs the best out of the 160 DE configurations for 5 dimensions.

Both DE-j-r2-B and DE-CoBi-r1-L show better performance than CMA-ES-pycma, CMA-ESwM, and cmaIH1e-1 for high dimensions and larger budgets of function evaluations.

#### Link to Data
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1PzABfbLLO2lcYaCU02DXdrqRROYs2MYb/view?usp=sharing

#### Optional: Source Code of Experiment
https://github.com/ryojitanabe/de_bbobmixint
